speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 20th of November, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Miriam Freedman. This is tape number 3. You were just talking about going into hiding, but you'd remembered something that you wanted to add from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The man who was a good friend of my uncle and aunt, Ella and Miklos Fisher, was Gerze Hoffman. Now, I understood he belonged to a Hlinka movement, which was a pro-Nazi movement. In spite of that, he did help us not to get on that train, what was quite a miracle to look later at what happened to all the Jews-- actually, a Nazi who saved us that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. If you'd like to continue with where we were, you were talking about the fact that you'd just met up with your aunts, and you were just there in the clothes that you stood in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So we entered that room-- [NON-ENGLISH] 52-- on a ground-floor flat which was empty. Now, obviously, people must have known what's going on. Like, Pernik, Gavalovic, they were working. Pernik was the caretaker of the block of flats. Gavalovic was-- William Gavalovic was a friend of my uncle who was an accountant in the mill as well. And I presume there were a few others-- director of the mill knew about-- or they devised a plan how to save my uncle and aunt. They did not plan to save eight people. But Ella was a very close member of her family. For some reason, now, she managed to persuade all these people that Lilly, Seren, Olga, Robby, Gertie, me should be included in this rescue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as day by day-- since it got worse, we could not find our father. Or at least our father did not reach that stage. And he may have known where we are, but we don't really know.

speaker: interviewee
text: All we know is that after a few days, my father appeared the other side of the road, unshaven, without any hat-- and kind of working clothes. And he probably tried to attract attention. Because we looked out the window, and he was shouting Olga, Olga! And I remember quite clearly my mother had-- used to-- a very high, [? pulled-- ?] choose to make a decision to go out to join my father or-- call him was impossible. Because it would have caused attention. And we did not really know. Maybe it was possible, but we didn't know how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my, my uncle and aunt must have given my, my aunt an-- a hint and tell her, look, if you go out, I don't know how you're going to come back. You're here. You're here. How are we going to get him in? We do not know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that was the end of my father. I never seen him anymore. That was the only time we saw him. And my mother, obviously, made a decision not to get out. I will never know exactly why she made the decision, and why we could not get him into the apartment. I really don't know why he could not be hinted with a finger or something near the curtain. But he didn't come. And that was the end. It was the last time that I seen-- we seen our father. Now, living in that room, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got ill. And a doctor was not able to-- you couldn't get a doctor. And I developed some boils around my body.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't really tell you what it was. But it was pretty bad. They had to squeeze it out, the, the pus? Pus, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was quite ill. And I do not know how I got this problem or this illness. I'd been living in that room for some time. And through the window, we saw some horrendous things, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you described them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a child I was sitting-- what could I do as a child? I was confined in a room, looking out the window, looking at what's going on. I was probably looking for my father, or looking, possibly, for somebody in-- a member of family. All I could see is that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one pregnant woman, they'd beaten up. Mothers with little babies in their-- hurry, hurry, hurry, hurry, pushing them, sort of, go faster! I don't know where they will be taken. But, like, it looks like madness. Is that-- a woman who's wheeling her baby in a pram was beaten up. She has to hurry up to go somewhere. Or a pregnant woman was rushed. And they were always Jews. And my mother recognized some of the people. I did not. Our town, town had quite number of Jews, but not tremendously. It was a little place, only a few thousand people, if that. But everybody knew everybody else. And I was always told to get away from the window. We couldn't wear shoes. And they must have decided. I suppose it was it was Pernik and Gavalovic, the other people who knew about our existence-- this is not a good place to be downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they managed to vacate a flat upstairs, second-floor flats. This was, so-called, the smartest block of flats in town at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a-- the part was for employees of the mill and the families. There were some single bachelor flats and some family flats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Upstairs, there was a single room for-- bachelor flat, what was a little entry hall, a living room with a gas ring, and a bathroom. And a young secretary lived there. And for-- they managed to convince her, persuade her to transfer to another company, another area. And the flat became vacant. But it still was on her name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved upstairs to that apartment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so-called-- we started to have some kind of life. And life-- we were sitting on the floor and doing nothing at that stage. So William and Helenka, they were smokers. And my aunt was-- one of my auntie was a dressmaker. So they brought us a bit of work to do. And they all were quite good with a needle, all the women. So that brought us some kind of occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these three kids-- Robby, Gertie, me-- were just sitting there, doing nothing, reading some magazines. We didn't have any books and no clothes. We had nothing, just what we wore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Pernik, who was the caretaker, was in charge of our physical being. They were really in charge of the hiding and also maybe supplying the money for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought the food to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pernik was a caretaker. And he had the greatest responsibility of all of them to look after our well-being. Now, he and his wife-- and she's still alive by the way, his wife, Maria. And they-- her mother as well-- they were living in an apartment downstairs. The caretakers used to live downstairs. He was in charge of the central heating, the basement. He was in charge of all the repairs and whatever is to be done. I think there were about 90 flats, maybe less. But that's what I think it was, maybe less. And he was in charge like most caretakers are. And he got us extras. They managed to persuade him to cooperate with this thing because they had to have him. There was no possibility to do without him. He was in charge of the food and the laundry and whatever else we needed physically. Maria, his wife, was doing the cooking. So I, I seen her-- I probably met her after the war. I didn't see her or her mother. And they had a little daughter, Duli, what was my age. And that was the caretaker family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to come morning, evening. He knew the-- when people are coming and going in that apartment. He knew when he could actually come upstairs to bring us some food, a little bit of news, and tell us when we can flush the toilet, when we can use the, the water. Because people-- the flats were very thin.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could hear upstairs-- somebody upstairs or downstairs could hear us if we used-- flushing the toilet. Anyway, they were suspicious later on about us being there. But-- so Pernik was giving us sort of orders, when to wash, when to do this, and when not to do that. And most of the time, we're just sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was that you actually went into this hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in August, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '94 or July-- the end of July or August, '94.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, I beg your pardon. 1944, yeah, that's right. And we stayed there until after the war about 10-11 months. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was just very, very dull at that time. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things were not straightforward. There was a basement. Now, all the continental flats used to have basement. And in the basement was the central heating. And also every flat had a little, confined part where they can keep the suitcases, boxes, things they don't use-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the laundry, the washing machine, or whatever they used to use were down in the basement. In our basement, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the narrow basement, Pernik carved a hole a little bit bigger than the fireplace. It seemed 4 foot by 6 foot. I still can't believe it. And in front of it, he put some shelves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the shelves, there would be jars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, things were becoming very bad. And I will explain to you soon. We would dash down into that hole and stay there for some time. And then, when things eased up a bit, we would go upstairs back to the apartment. Now, by this time, hardly any Jews in the town. All the Jews had been taken away. A loudspeaker-- and we could hear the loudspeaker in our apartment-- loudspeaker all over the place. Anybody hiding a Jew, his life is in danger. Anybody who hides a Jew, a thousand Krone for a reward.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could hear it all. The temptation was tremendous. Small town, small place-- we hear these loudspeakers all over the place. They're going to clear the town of Jews. There were not going to be any more Jews left here. And they are a menace to society, and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the temptation was very great for Pernik, for Gavalovic, for anybody, because the-- our risk is that we're all terribly young. The lady, Helena-- Helenka, who saved us, she was in her early 20s. And Maria was probably two years older. They were all very, very young women who could have been tempted and didn't have much money. Helena was a secretary. And so the temptation was quite there. But also on the other hand, there was also loyalty to friends-- to their friends as well. Now, from time to time, because it was a very nice building, German officers wanted to have apartments. When they saw-- coming now, it was quite occupied-- our city was occupied by Germans. The whole city has taken-- the administration already taken over. They were looking for apartments. And, of course, the officer will go to a place they want to live. It is a nice, respectable-- it was near the river, a nice block of flats.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from time to time, the rumor came around that there's a few apartments. The secretaries are not here. And there's apartment here and an apartment there. And they went to the mill and asked.

speaker: interviewee
text: When this came about, the head-- the caretaker had to show them to everybody, to these officers, the apartment. So we dash us down quickly, between-- they were making appointment the following morning. So we dashed down into that hole. And that was quite a tricky things. Because people upstairs were suspicious about us already-- and people below us. One of the people next to us was told about it. He emigrated his family. They had to tell one or two people they could sort of so-called trust. The next-door apartment would be, like, from here to the kitchen door, he was told about it. And he managed to get his whole family out. We never seen him. But he knew about our existence. But there was some fellow upstairs or downstairs who must have notified from time to time the Germans that Jews in this building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first incident I tried to tell you is because the rumor came that there are some empty flats here. So we would dash down. And the journey down was very complicated. Gavalovic was taking one of my aunts. They're all very pretty women, very attractive women, all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girlfriend, boyfriend dashing down-- lovers going down for a walk. So they had to dress them somehow and go down into the cellar. And there were a few men available. There was Pernik available. There was Gavalovic and my uncle. And there were sort of-- and four woman. But they managed to get down. And I was taken down in a laundry bag, a laundry basket. I was very small. I was-- weighed three or four stones. On, on, on one occasion, I was coming-- walking down. But I remember being taken down as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What will they do? They would sort of quickly move the furniture out, garbage it, put shit everywhere possible, and make the place really unlivable. And when the officer will come to see it, he would say, my, some very bad lodgers or irrespective people didn't treat the place properly. And so they were put off.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as they have been-- the German officer were put off, the place was cleaned up by the caretaker and his wife. And we were brought back. And that would last for a long time. And that was pretty hard down in that hole. I do have good memories. If I tell you, it was bigger than this fireplace. A little bit deeper, double the depths of this fireplace. The height was probably up till here, [POINTS] a little taller than this. I still-- when I go down to see it-- you saw the photograph. And I went to see I can't still believe it. So obviously, we're sitting like sardines like that, squeezing on each other to keep each other warm. Because there was a pipe running on the edge. I think it was a central-heating pipe. And I was frightened to death. I was always-- pressing on me. I was pushed in the corner. I was the youngest. And we were sitting there. On the outside was the little bucket. So when it was quiet, we'd come out and then do that in the bucket. There was a washroom. They used it as well as a toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you stand up in this hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no. You could hardly sit. You couldn't sit at all. But what is very strange-- that if you would have coughed-- because other people can go to the cellar to use it for whatever they want to. Some people use it as a carpentry, repairs. If you cough or sneeze, you would be caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave us some very, very large eiderdowns, like quilts. And if you-- you can actually try it out. You can cough through it. You don't-- it doesn't-- the echo doesn't come through sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would become terribly bad, this. Worse, it was that the basement was quite big. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a group of-- one part of the mill, a group of soldiers occupied. And they used the basement for training. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we were in that hole on a few occasions, the holes were not closed because we need a bit of air. But they came down to train sometimes for two or three hours. One of us would have done just-- this, looking back at it-- just sneeze or cough or clear the through or something, we'd be caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, they were shouting and talking through the exercises. But they didn't do it all the time. So this was our most dangerous and most fearful times. Because it is just really good that we have been not-- we haven't been caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were in the hole, was there any food at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the training was finished, Pernik used to come in the evening. I just thought-- think back that the women-- and as we had to use the toilet, I don't really remember any accidents. Obviously, we didn't eat so much. So you don't really have to go out eliminate yourself when you don't eat proper meals. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Pernik used to come down quite late in the evening. He knew the whereabouts of everything. He was like a spy, watching who's coming, when they went away, when he was-- it was quiet. He had a suitcase. I'll never forget his suitcase. People became suspicious of his suitcase, of all of his food. He used to come down. He took us out to this little cellar, out of the hole. And there we had our food, and whatever or not. We hardly ever washed ourselves properly. And then toward the night, we had to go back again. Then he stayed there for half an hour, 20 minutes, feeding us. And then he's-- we finished our food. And we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend in that hole in 24 hours, in every 24 hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's truly big. I think we stayed all the time, sometimes. I don't think-- until the evening-- went out, something-- I, I sometimes look at it. It's just not possible to visualize it if you had seen the size of it. We just sat like sardines. We didn't move. We didn't think. And I think of-- I thought, I'm going to suffocate. I always remember this big quilt on top of me. And that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: We whispered if we had to. I mean, we knew ourselves if somebody was coming. Because, you see, it was all stone floor. So obviously, if-- we were like hawks, just waiting to listen. If anybody comes, we stopped. I just can't believe it that we didn't catch a cold, or we didn't get any temperature or illnesses, and that we were protected. Somebody very high-- somebody was looking after us. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, I'm going to tell you that the greatest incident is that we survived. So things-- so we went down. And then we went upstairs. And then we went downstairs. And we had a little occupation upstairs, working. And I was reading women's magazines. There was no-- nothing to study or read anything. On one incident, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Pernik said he cannot help us anymore. We depended on him completely. He said a German, drunken soldier walked in. And he was told that there were Jews in this building. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was insisting that he is going to find them. And he knows they're here. He got-- first, he got information from somebody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I presumed Maria, his wife, gave the Germans some more drink. He dashed upstairs. We were on the second floor. So he couldn't take us down this time to the cellar because it was too dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: It takes 10-15 minutes-- all of us to get down the cellar. He couldn't take us down to the cellar. So he said, well, you stay in the apartment, and you can decide to do anything you like. There was a little gas ring. You can open the gas. He could always pretend they did not know somebody was here. You can do whatever you like. But I cannot do anything anymore. This is-- he said, the German came. He took all my keys. And he told me, you're coming with me from one apartment to another apartment. They're going to thoroughly look through, search. They're going to find them. And that's-- he said goodbye to us. And that was that. He went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Miklos, my uncle, had to make a decision to put on the gas ring-- on. Because we didn't-- Pernik said, if you die, there is this fire going on in the basement. It can burn you or something or another. There was an incinerator going. A big one, you know, for coal fire for the central heating. He said-- so my uncle had to make a decision. Should we open the gas ring or not? But you know, when you open the gas, it also takes a few hours till you die. You don't die just the minute that you put it on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had been a very mischievous child, or a child who wanted to live. I always think I had some work to do with my life. I can't do this. And my uncle, He put on the gas. I opened the window to jump out of the window. I preferred to die like that. I do remember that quite strongly.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't tell you if my saying that or from other spirit or something came over it, but he didn't put on the gas ring, right. And then, we did not know. Shall we go hide behind the wardrobe? It all looked so very, very stupid because it was a small room. Shall we go behind the wardrobe, all of us, and hide there or whatever not? And then it-- of course, dispose of all the bits of things we had around. In the end, my feeling was, we surrender to the idea that we're going to be caught. It was like-- I do remember this so strongly that it was accepted that we are going to be caught. And that is that. How long had you been in hiding at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose eight months or nine months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I didn't say-- tell you earlier that Pernik changed the lock of our door in the early stages because some people were suspicious. And we had an incident. A fellow upstairs or somebody downstairs tried to-- maybe the girlfriend-- maybe somebody who had a relation with the girl, we do not know. So the lock has been changed. It was a special key. But when he took the German around, for some reason, consciously or unconsciously, I could never test it, he gave him the old set of keys-- the previous lock we had on the door. So what's happened now is the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went from flat to flat. And now we can hear him going to the next door apartment to the man who knew about us. And then the singing. Oh, it was quite merry, quite loud. They're talking and everything. And everybody gave a bit more to drink. They gave them more and more to drink. And now we are sitting here, utterly shivering with fear, with anxiety. We knew that any minute comes the end of it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we're just waiting. And now, we can hear somebody puts a key into the lock of our door. There's a commotion-- the gentleman next door, and Pernik and this German-- commotion. We can hear the key in the keyhole. And we can hear the noises, the rattling of the key. We can hear everything. And we know that any second-- but the key wouldn't go through. Because, I think, that he changed the keyhole. And he struggled. And then I-- we could hear another commotion, another commotion. And all of a sudden, it's quiet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They walked away. And we just sit. We don't know what's going on. What's going to happen? Is there going to come more people in, or whatever is going to happen? We don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a couple of hours later, Pernik walks in. And he says, you've been saved. He said, he insisted to enter into every apartment. There was not a-- but he tore it to pieces, every apartment. And when we reached your apartment, I told him we just walked out of it. He tried to put the key in and the key wasn't going through, whatever it was. He said we just came out. He gave him a bit more to drink. And he accepted it. For some particular reason, he accepted it. And that's the only apartment he didn't go in. And that's how we were saved that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember exactly when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I [SIGHS] remember this because it was so powerful. It was such a-- this is a-- probably the most powerful experience because I was a child. And I wanted to live. And I did not know-- we had to sit there in utter silence. And the feeling is that we were told we were caught. And we can hear, actually, the German speaking. We could actually hear the rattling of the key. We can hear that someone tries to open the, the door. I mean, it was so powerful, we just could not understand that the keyhole-- that the key did not open. And that, that we could not understand. We did not know that the key has been switched. And we did not know that Pernik managed to talk him into it, that we came out of his apartment. He was so drunk. Because in the apartment next door, they sat very long time. They must have probably finished a bottle of vodka or Slivovitz, or whatever they were drinking. I think he-- they must have just poured all the time more and more drink into him because that was the only way they could hope that they can confuse him. Even he believed it was a miracle. And we all believed it was a miracle. There was just not any other explanation because they didn't miss any apartment. And the feeling was-- I just remember the whole thing very clearly like today-- the rattling of the key in the keyhole.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of another tape.
